topic: cafe bean sourcing — single origin Ethiopian

turns.content: ok so we just got these new single origin ethiopian beans at work and i am genuinely losing my mind over them

Oh nice! Ethiopian single origins are some of my favorite coffees to talk about. Do you know which region they're from? Ethiopia has such a wide range of flavor profiles depending on where the beans are grown.

yoirgacheffe!! like classic yirgacheffe washed process. the tasting notes say blueberry and jasmine and i was like ok sure thats marketing speak... but no. it genuinely tastes like blueberries?? how does a coffee bean taste like blueberries this is witchcraft

Yirgacheffe is the real deal! Those blueberry notes aren't marketing fluff — it's actually one of the most distinctive flavor profiles in specialty coffee. The combination of the high altitude (1,700-2,200m), the specific heirloom varietals grown in that region, and the washed processing method all contribute.

The washed process especially brings out those bright, fruity, floral notes because it strips away the fruit mucilage before drying, so you get a really clean cup that lets those natural berry compounds shine. The jasmine/floral thing is typical of Yirgacheffe too — it's almost tea-like.

Are you pulling it as espresso or doing filter?

were doing both but honestly as a pour over its unreal. like i made one for myself on my break and just sat there having a spiritual experience in the back room. as espresso its a bit more intense and the acidity is heaps more pronounced which some people love but i reckon it shines as filter

That tracks — lighter, fruitier single origins often do their best work as pour over because you get that full spectrum of flavor without the intensity of espresso compressing everything. The acidity that's "bright and juicy" in a pour over can become "sharp and aggressive" as a shot if the grind and dose aren't dialed in perfectly.

Are your customers vibing with it? Some people hear "fruity coffee" and get skeptical because they're expecting that classic dark roast bitterness.

its so polarising lol. the specialty coffee nerds are losing it in a good way and ordering multiple cups. but then you get the people who take one sip and go 'this doesnt taste like coffee' and im like... it IS coffee its just not burnt??? the number of people who think coffee is supposed to taste like charcoal is genuinely distressing

Ha, the "this doesn't taste like coffee" reaction is such a classic specialty coffee moment. It's like telling someone who's only ever had instant that there's a whole world out there.

To be fair, most people's reference point for coffee IS that dark, bitter, roasty profile because that's what commercial coffee has trained them on. Light roast single origins are basically a different beverage to those palates. It takes a bit of exposure to recalibrate.

true true. i lowkey love being the person who converts someone though. like when a regular whos been ordering their standard large latte for months finally tries a filter pour over and their face does that thing where they go '...oh'. thats the good stuff. thats why i do this job. that and the money obviously lol

The conversion moment is genuinely special — watching someone's entire concept of coffee shift in real time. That's the barista equivalent of a teacher's "lightbulb moment."

Do you know how long you'll have the Yirgacheffe? Some cafes rotate their single origins pretty frequently.

i think we got like a 10kg bag so probably a few weeks depending on how fast it moves. im hoping my boss orders more cos the last single origin we had was a colombian that was fine but kind of boring compared to this. like it was good but it didnt make me want to write poetry about it you know

Colombian coffees can be super solid and balanced but yeah, they rarely have that "this is a completely different experience" factor that a good Ethiopian brings. They're the reliable workhorse vs the exciting wildcard.

If your boss is open to suggestions and this one sells well, it's worth pushing for more African single origins in the rotation — Kenyan beans can have a similar wow factor with those bright, complex fruit notes. But enjoy the Yirgacheffe while it lasts!

ooh kenyan is a good shout actually ill mention that. also do you reckon the roast level matters heaps for this kind of bean? cos i feel like if someone did a dark roast on these yirgacheffe beans it would literally be a crime. like you would destroy everything thats good about them

Absolutely — dark roasting Yirgacheffe beans would be borderline criminal. All those delicate floral and fruit compounds are volatile, so they break down at higher roast temperatures. What you'd end up with is basically just carbon and bitterness, and you could've done that with any cheap commodity bean. The whole point of paying for a premium single origin is to taste what makes it unique, and a dark roast just erases all of that.

Light to medium-light is really the sweet spot for most Ethiopian coffees. Just enough roast development to bring out the sweetness without steamrolling the origin character.

exactly!! ok i need to stop talking about coffee and actually go do my readings but i reckon i just needed to tell someone about the blueberry thing because none of my friends care this much about beans lol. thanks for nerding out with me

Ha, sometimes you just need someone who appreciates a good bean moment. The blueberry thing is genuinely exciting so your enthusiasm is completely valid. Good luck with the readings — and enjoy converting more customers to the light side!
